g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Queues, a fundamental FIFO data structure essential for ordered data processing.
g.text: Queue Data Structure - FIFO Operations & Types

g.data-narration: A queue is a linear data structure following FIFO - First-In-First-Out principle. Elements are inserted at rear, removed from front.
Queues mirror real-world waiting lines: first person in line gets served first, like customers at a ticket counter.
Queues have five key characteristics: ordered collection, FIFO principle, two operational ends, dynamic size, and single access point.
Five fundamental queue operations: enqueue adds to rear, dequeue removes from front, peek views front, isEmpty and isFull check status.
Watch the step-by-step queue operations: enqueue adds 10, 20, 30 sequentially, then dequeue returns 10 (first element out).
Queue array implementation uses queue[] array, MAX size, and two indices: front and rear tracking element positions.
Enqueue operation adds an element at the rear. When first element added, front is set to 0. Returns overflow if queue is full.
Dequeue removes from front, returns the element, then advances front index. Returns underflow if queue is empty.
Linear queue suffers memory wastage: after dequeues, front advances but rear reaches end, preventing new insertions.
g.text: In linear queue, after dequeues, cannot reuse empty slots at front

g.data-narration: Circular queue solves wastage by wrapping rear to front, reusing empty slots efficiently in a ring-like structure.
g.text: Rear wraps around to beginning when it reaches end - reuses empty slots

g.data-narration: Four queue types: Linear (basic FIFO), Circular (ring buffer), Deque (double-ended), Priority (priority-based removal).
